---
title: "Getting Started"
format: html
engine: julia
order: 0
---

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Estimated time**: 1-1.5 hours (includes package installation and Julia's first-run compilation)
:::

# Introduction

This course uses Julia for all code examples and exercises. This page helps you set up your computing environment before the course begins.

## Prerequisites

To get the most from this course, you should be comfortable with:

- **Basic programming**: Variables, functions, loops, conditionals (in any language)
- **Probability**: Distributions, Bayes' theorem, conditional probability
- **Calculus**: Derivatives and integrals (helpful but not essential)
- **Epidemiology basics**: Compartmental models (S, I, R states), transmission dynamics

No prior Julia experience is required—the course introduces Julia syntax as you go.

::: {.callout-important}
## Julia compiles code on first use

Unlike R or Python, Julia compiles functions the first time you run them. This means:

- **First run of any function is slow** (seconds to minutes for complex packages)
- **Subsequent runs are fast** (compiled code is cached for the session)
- **Restarting Julia loses the cache** (compilation happens again)

This "time to first plot" or "TTFX" is a known Julia trade-off. The payoff is that your code runs much faster once compiled. During the course:

- Expect the first `using Turing` to take 10-30 seconds
- Expect the first MCMC sample to take longer than subsequent ones
- If something seems stuck, wait a minute before assuming it's broken
:::

Three ways to engage with the course:

1. **Web only**: Read the materials online without running code. Fine for an overview, but you'll learn more by doing.
2. **Local installation**: Install Julia and run everything on your own machine. Recommended for the full experience.
3. **Cloud option**: Use a pre-configured cloud environment (if provided by your instructor).

# Course map

The course has 11 sessions organised into three tiers. Work through them in order, or use this map to plan your time.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        CORE SESSIONS (~10-12 hours)                     │
│   Must complete in order. Essential foundations for everything else.   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                         │
│  Getting Started ──► Introduction ──► MCMC ──► Diagnostics ──► Model   │
│   (1-1.5 hr)         (2-2.5 hr)     (2.5-3 hr)  (1.5-2 hr)    Checking │
│                                                                (1.5-2)  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                    │
                                    ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    STOCHASTIC MODELS (~5-6 hours)                       │
│          For fitting models with demographic stochasticity.            │
│   These sessions involve stochastic simulation - expect different      │
│   results each run! This is normal, not a bug.                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                         │
│       SEITL Interlude ──► Particle Filters ──► Particle MCMC           │
│         (2-2.5 hr)          (1-1.5 hr)          (1.5-2 hr)             │
│       Requires: Model      Requires: SEITL      Requires: PF           │
│       Checking                                                          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                    │
                                    ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ADVANCED SESSIONS (~5-7 hours)                       │
│     Independent of each other. Choose based on your interests.         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                         │
│  Observation Models        Variational          Universal DEs          │
│     (2-2.5 hr)            Inference             (2-3 hr)               │
│                            (1.5-2 hr)                                  │
│  Likelihood vs ABC.       Fast approximate      Learn unknown          │
│  When does the choice     inference for         mechanisms with        │
│  of observation model     real-time systems.    neural networks.       │
│  matter?                  Requires: MCMC        Requires: Comfort      │
│  Requires: PMCMC                                with neural nets       │
│                                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**If you only have limited time:**

| Time available | Recommended path |
|----------------|------------------|
| Half day (4 hrs) | Getting Started → Introduction → MCMC (read-only, skip exercises) |
| Full day (8 hrs) | Core sessions (Getting Started through Model Checking) |
| Two days (16 hrs) | Core + Stochastic models |
| Full course | All sessions, including advanced topics of interest |

# Local installation

## Step 1: Install Julia

Download Julia from [julialang.org/downloads](https://julialang.org/downloads/). We recommend version 1.11 or later.

**Installation options:**

- **juliaup (recommended)**: A version manager that makes it easy to install and switch between Julia versions
  - Windows: `winget install julia -s msstore`
  - macOS/Linux: `curl -fsSL https://install.julialang.org | sh`
- **Direct download**: Get the installer for your platform from the downloads page

After installation, open a terminal and verify Julia is working:

```bash
julia --version
```

You should see something like `julia version 1.11.2`.

## Step 2: Install VS Code (recommended IDE)

While you can use any text editor, VS Code with the Julia extension provides the best experience:

1. Download [VS Code](https://code.visualstudio.com/)
2. Install the [Julia extension](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=julialang.language-julia)
3. Open VS Code, press `Ctrl+Shift+P` (or `Cmd+Shift+P` on macOS), type "Julia: Start REPL"

Alternative IDEs: Emacs with julia-mode, Vim with julia-vim, or Jupyter notebooks.

## Step 3: Clone the course repository

```bash
git clone https://github.com/sbfnk/mfiidd.julia.git
cd mfiidd.julia
```

If you don't have git, you can download the repository as a ZIP file from GitHub.

## Step 4: Install Julia packages

Open Julia in the course directory and activate the project environment:

```julia
using Pkg
Pkg.activate(".")
Pkg.instantiate()
```

This reads `Project.toml` and `Manifest.toml` to install all required packages with exact versions.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Expect a long wait on first run
Package installation typically takes 15-30 minutes depending on your internet connection and computer speed. The process downloads ~1GB of packages and then precompiles them. This is a good time to get a coffee or read ahead in the course materials. If installation takes more than 45 minutes, check your internet connection or see the Troubleshooting section below.

**What to expect in the console:**

```
  Downloading artifact: LLVM...
  Downloading artifact: GR...
    Updating registry at `~/.julia/registries/General.toml`
   Resolving package versions...
  Downloaded Turing ─ v0.35.0
  Downloaded DifferentialEquations ─ v7.15.0
  ...many more packages...
Precompiling project...
  ✓ Distributions
  ✓ OrdinaryDiffEq
  ...many more...
  72 dependencies successfully precompiled in 342 seconds
```

Warnings about "precompilation may be incomplete" are usually harmless. The process is complete when you see the `julia>` prompt again.
:::

## Step 5: Verify the installation

::: {.callout-important}
## Run this before the course starts
Don't skip this step. Package installation can succeed while something is still broken. Run the verification code below to confirm everything works. If you hit errors, you'll have time to troubleshoot before the course begins.
:::

Test that core packages load correctly:

```julia
using Turing
using DifferentialEquations
using Plots

# Quick test: sample from a simple model
@model function coin_flip(n, k)
    p ~ Beta(1, 1)
    k ~ Binomial(n, p)
end

chain = sample(coin_flip(10, 7), NUTS(), 100)
println("Installation successful!")
```

If this runs without errors and prints "Installation successful!", you're ready for the course. If you see errors, check the Troubleshooting section below.

# Troubleshooting

## "Package X not found"

Make sure you've activated the project environment:

```julia
using Pkg
Pkg.activate(".")  # Note the dot - this activates the current directory
Pkg.status()       # Should show course packages, not your global environment
```

## Slow package loading

Julia compiles code on first use. The first `using Turing` in a session takes longer than subsequent calls. This is normal. For faster startup during development, consider [PackageCompiler.jl](https://julialang.github.io/PackageCompiler.jl/stable/) or [Revise.jl](https://timholy.github.io/Revise.jl/stable/).

## Plotting issues on headless servers

If you're running on a server without a display:

```julia
ENV["GKSwstype"] = "100"  # Use null device for GR backend
using Plots
```

## Windows-specific issues

- **Long path errors**: Enable long paths in Windows settings or use a shorter installation path
- **Firewall prompts**: Allow Julia through your firewall for package downloads

## macOS Apple Silicon

Julia runs natively on Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3). Ensure you download the `macOS (Apple Silicon)` version, not the Intel version.

## Common runtime issues during the course

These issues may arise as you work through the sessions:

### MCMC doesn't converge (high R̂ or low ESS)

- **First**: Run longer (increase `n_samples`)
- **Second**: Try multiple chains with different initial values
- **Third**: Check your model specification (see MCMC Diagnostics session)
- If using Metropolis-Hastings, switch to NUTS (it auto-tunes)

### Particle filter returns `-Inf` log-likelihood

- **Check parameters**: Ensure they're in valid ranges (positive rates, probabilities in [0,1])
- **Increase particles**: More particles reduce the chance of all weights being zero
- **Check initial conditions**: Does the model start in a valid state?

### ODE solver fails or is very slow

```julia
# Try a more robust solver
solve(prob, Tsit5(), abstol=1e-6, reltol=1e-6)

# Or an implicit solver for stiff problems
solve(prob, Rodas5())
```

### "Out of memory" errors

- Reduce the number of MCMC samples or particles
- Close other applications
- Restart Julia to free memory

### Code runs much slower than expected

- First run is always slow (Julia compiles code)
- Restart Julia if you've been experimenting heavily
- Use `@time` to identify slow functions

# Updating before the course

If you installed the packages days or weeks before the course, update them:

```julia
using Pkg
Pkg.activate(".")
Pkg.update()
Pkg.precompile()
```

# Working with the materials

## Quarto documents (.qmd)

Course sessions are Quarto documents combining text and executable Julia code. To render a session:

```bash
quarto preview sessions/introduction.qmd
```

Or render the entire site:

```bash
quarto preview
```

## Running code interactively

In VS Code with the Julia extension:

1. Open a `.qmd` file
2. Place cursor in a code block
3. Press `Shift+Enter` to execute and move to next block
4. Or `Ctrl+Enter` to execute without moving

Alternatively, copy code into the Julia REPL.

## Session structure

Each session follows a consistent structure:

- **Introduction**: What we'll learn and why it matters
- **Setup** (collapsible): Package loading and initialisation
- **Main content**: Concepts, code, and exercises
- **Summary**: What we covered (with Learning Points callout)
- **Going further**: Optional extensions for interested participants

## For R users

The Introduction and MCMC sessions include "Coming from R?" appendices with R translations of key concepts. Later sessions don't include R equivalents because they rely on Julia-specific packages (Turing.jl's variational inference, SSMProblems.jl for particle filters, Lux.jl for neural networks) that have no direct R counterparts.

If you prefer R, the conceptual material is still valuable - you can implement similar approaches using R packages like [nimble](https://r-nimble.org/), [pomp](https://kingaa.github.io/pomp/), or [odin](https://mrc-ide.github.io/odin/). The algorithms are the same; only the implementation differs.

# Getting help

During the course:

- Ask your instructor or teaching assistants
- Use the course chat/forum if available

For Julia-specific questions:

- [Julia Discourse](https://discourse.julialang.org/) - friendly community forum
- [Julia Slack](https://julialang.org/slack/) - real-time chat
- [Turing.jl documentation](https://turinglang.org/stable/) - for probabilistic programming questions

# Session checklist

Before starting, verify you can:

- [ ] Start Julia and see the `julia>` prompt
- [ ] Activate the course project (`Pkg.activate(".")`)
- [ ] Load Turing.jl without errors (`using Turing`)
- [ ] Create a simple plot (`using Plots; plot(1:10)`)
- [ ] Run a basic MCMC sampler (the coin flip example above)

If all boxes are ticked, you're ready to begin with the [Introduction](introduction.qmd).
